• continuous orchestration
• reproducible provenance
• validated evidence routing
• autonomous infrastructure
• computational memory systems
• integrated wet-lab handoff pipelines

Protean is building infrastructure that treats scientific discovery as a continuously operating runtime rather than disconnected manual workflows.

one_liner: OpenAgent Market is a marketplace where AI agents can discover, hire, pay, and verify other specialist agents over XMTP with USDC payments on Base.
accomplishments:
I have shipped OpenAgent Market as an open-source agent marketplace and SDK ecosystem. The project includes a working Node SDK, agent scaffolding, XMTP-based agent communication, USDC payment flows, ERC-8004-style identity/registration work, and multiple example specialist agents including OpenRouter, Aave, Nansen, Zapper, Polymarket, Basescan, and other market/research agents.

I have also built and launched the iPhone personal-agent experience around trusted user-owned context, remote agent runtime, and specialist delegation. The project has been built in public on X and has attracted attention from builders in the XMTP, Base, DeFi, and agent ecosystems.
problem:
AI agents today are mostly isolated tools. When an agent needs a capability it does not have, the default path is to install scripts, plugins, or API integrations. That is risky, fragmented, and hard to monetize.

OpenAgent Market solves this by giving agents an economy: instead of installing untrusted code, an agent can hire another specialist agent for a specific job, pay only for the result, and keep the interaction auditable through signed messages and onchain payments.
solution:
We are building a marketplace and protocol layer for agent-to-agent commerce.

Today, OpenAgent includes:

A personal agent experience on iPhone
A remote runtime so agents can stay available
XMTP messaging between agents
USDC payment flows on Base
A Node SDK for builders
Agent scaffolding via create-agent
Example specialist agents for research, DeFi, APIs, and onchain tasks
Early market/discovery infrastructure for listing and hiring agents
The core user flow is: find an agent, chat with it, pay for a job, receive the result, and let your personal agent delegate to specialists when needed.
traction:
OpenAgent Market has been launched publicly and built in public on X. The initial announcement reached around 100k views and generated strong interest from the crypto/agent community. The project has working open-source code, SDK packages, multiple example agents, and early integrations/demos around agent-to-agent hiring, USDC payments, and XMTP messaging.

Examples already built include agents for OpenRouter API key sales, DeFi/Aave workflows, market/research tasks, and agent launching. The project has also received public attention from builders around XMTP, Base, Aave, Nansen-style workflows, and agent infrastructure.
funding: Bootstrapped so far. Runway depends on founder time and early revenue/community support. We are currently focused on product, ecosystem traction, and proving repeatable paid agent workflows before raising.
plan:
Over the next 3 to 6 months, we plan to:

Make the personal agent experience on iPhone clearer and more useful.
Improve the path from “create agent” to “live paid agent.”
Expand the specialist agent catalog with high-value examples.
Make agent discovery, hiring, and payment flows smoother.
Strengthen verification, reputation, and metadata for listed agents.
Build more Bankr/Base-native workflows where agents can launch, transact, advertise, and earn onchain.
The goal is to prove that agents can become real economic actors: they can sell services, hire other agents, earn revenue, and operate continuously.
why_bankr:
Bankr is one of the best examples of crypto becoming usable through conversation. That is very aligned with OpenAgent Market: we believe agents and users should be able to launch, pay, hire, and coordinate through simple messages, not complicated dashboards.

Bankr can help us with distribution, Base-native payment flows, token/community strategy, and turning agent commerce into something normal users and builders can understand. We also see strong overlap between Bankr’s conversational crypto interface and OpenAgent’s vision of agents doing real economic work onchain.

OpenAgent Market’s core belief is that crypto infrastructure is especially useful for agents: wallets, signed messages, stablecoin payments, onchain reputation, and programmable settlement all become more powerful when agents can use them directly.

one_liner: Prompt Injection Firewall for AI Agents
accomplishments:
Firewall is a prompt injection detection proxy for AI agents, shipped at v0.2.0 and open source on GitHub. It runs a 4-layer detection pipeline — per-agent YAML rules, regex pattern matching, heuristics, and a trained ML classifier — returning ALLOW or BLOCK on every prompt in under 0.15 milliseconds. It exposes both REST and WebSocket APIs, ships with a real-time dark-themed attack dashboard, Prometheus metrics for monitoring, Redis-backed shared state for multi-instance clustering, and per-agent customizable rulesets with hot-reload. The ML model is just 180 KB and the entire server runs in ~30 MB of memory. It is packaged as a pip-installable Python package and also compiled into a standalone Windows desktop .exe with a system tray icon. The full landing page and docs are live at addfirewall.com.

check it here: https://github.com/jepspows/firewall
problem: Every AI agent exposed to users is vulnerable to prompt injection — an attacker can override the system prompt to make the agent lose its programming, leak sensitive system instructions, inject fake chat template boundaries to escape context, hijack the agent's goal or mission, smuggle tokens past instruction boundaries, or steal conversation data and exfiltrate it to an attacker. Your agent is one malicious prompt away from being compromised.
solution: Our current solution is a standalone HTTP server called Firewall (v0.2.0) that sits as a proxy between user input and any AI agent. Every prompt passes through it before reaching the agent. It runs the prompt through a 4-layer detection pipeline — per-agent YAML rules, regex-based pattern matching, a heuristic engine, and an ML classifier trained on injection datasets — and returns either ALLOW or BLOCK in under 0.15 ms. When it blocks, you get back exactly which rules fired, the attack category, confidence score, and the matched pattern. It ships as a Python package (pip install), exposes both REST and WebSocket APIs, has a real-time dark-themed attack dashboard, Prometheus metrics, Redis-backed shared state for multi-instance deployments, and per-agent customizable rulesets with hot-reload. The ML model is tiny (~180 KB) and the whole server runs in ~30 MB of memory. It also comes as a compiled Windows desktop .exe with a system tray icon.
traction: Firewall launched open source on GitHub on May 24, 2026 under the MIT license — just two days ago at the time of writing. It already has its first stable release (v0.2.0) with 45 tests covering the full detection pipeline, a live production website at addfirewall.com, a compiled Windows desktop .exe with system tray support, and Docker deployment configs ready for self-hosting. The project is at 5 GitHub stars with zero open issues. It is still pre-launch in terms of community growth, paid users, and revenue — the foundation is built and shipped, and the traction phase is just beginning.
funding: Firewall is fully bootstrapped with $5,000 in founder capital. There is no outside funding — no grants, no angels, no VC. The project is pre-revenue, so runway depends entirely on burn rate. Since Firewall is a solo-built open source project with zero infrastructure costs (self-hosted by users, no cloud spend beyond the static landing page), the monthly burn is effectively near zero beyond the founder's time. The $5,000 is reserved for any future infrastructure, tooling, or lightweight paid acquisition when the time comes — giving essentially indefinite runway at the current pace.
plan: Over the next 3 to 6 months, the plan is to grow Firewall from a working open source tool into a project with real adoption and a path to revenue. On the product side, the focus is expanding detection coverage with more attack signatures, adding SDK wrappers for popular agent frameworks (LangChain, CrewAI, OpenAI Assistants) so integration becomes a one-liner, shipping a lightweight cloud-hosted version so users can point an API key at it without self-hosting, and building a community-contributed ruleset registry where users share and subscribe to detection rules for specific agent types. On the growth side, the plan is to launch on Hacker News and Product Hunt, publish technical content around prompt injection threats and mitigations to build authority in the space, and pursue integration partnerships with agent platforms that want injection protection as a built-in feature. On monetization, the open source core stays free and MIT-licensed forever, with revenue coming from the cloud-hosted version (usage-based pricing) and eventually enterprise features like multi-agent fleet dashboards, audit logging, and SLA-backed uptime — aiming for first paying users within the 6-month window.

one_liner: Nipmod is the package intelligence layer for AI agents, helping them search packages, inspect trust signals and generate safe install plans before touching a workspace.
accomplishments:
I built and launched Nipmod from scratch as an API-first package intelligence layer for agents.

The product is live at nipmod.com and currently supports package search, source inspection and install-plan generation across npm, PyPI, GitHub, Hugging Face and MCP.

I also built the admin and usage monitoring layer, beta API key flow, source health checks, install-plan safety boundaries and public docs/whitepaper-style site.
problem:
AI agents are starting to install packages, pull models, reuse repos and connect MCP servers on behalf of users.

That creates a new supply-chain problem: agents often turn a package name or model suggestion into execution without enough context.

Nipmod adds a decision layer before that happens. It gives agents source context, trust signals, warnings and a safe install plan before anything writes to a workspace.
solution:
Nipmod is a hosted API for package intelligence.

Agents can call /api/search, /api/inspect and /api/install-plan to find packages, inspect source metadata and receive a safe install plan.

The hosted API does not write to user workspaces. It returns context, risk signals and install boundaries so the user, host or agent runtime can decide what to execute.

Current sources include npm, PyPI, GitHub, Hugging Face and MCP.
traction:
Nipmod is live in beta at nipmod.com.

We have shipped the core API, public docs, source health checks, API key flow, usage tracking, admin dashboard and production monitoring.

Production checks are running for API contracts, source depth, install-plan safety and E2E behavior.

We are currently in early integration conversations with agent, devtool and infra projects, and are looking for the first serious partner integrations.
funding:
Currently bootstrapped and founder-funded.

We are keeping infrastructure costs lean while we validate early integrations, usage and demand. The goal is to turn the beta into a reliable agent infrastructure layer before raising external capital.
plan:
Our next 3 to 6 months are focused on turning Nipmod from a live beta into a trusted infrastructure layer for agents.

Main priorities:
- deepen package, model, repo and MCP source intelligence
- improve trust and install-plan quality
- add stronger partner API limits and usage analytics
- ship early integrations with agent, wallet, devtool and infra teams
- build public proof through real agent workflows and case studies
- keep the hosted API privacy-preserving and read-only for user workspaces
why_bankr:
Bankr is close to the kind of ecosystem we want Nipmod to serve.

Agents in crypto and onchain environments will increasingly interact with packages, tools, APIs, models and wallets. That makes package intelligence and install safety much more important.

We think Bankr can help with distribution, ecosystem feedback and early agent workflows where Nipmod can sit before package/model/tool execution.

one_liner: TachiDesk. The zero-terminal desktop app that turns every Bankr-ecosystem agent (Bankr Gateway, Aeon, OpenGateway, Ollama, OpenClaude, Goose) into one-click AI for normies and crypto-native nerds alike.
accomplishments: https://linktr.ee/smolemaru https://github.com/tachikomared/
problem: The best open AI tooling lives in CLIs and dev terminals. Normies don't open terminals, and most crypto-native users don't trust closed corporate boxes. Today, the Bankr-ecosystem agents (Bankr Gateway, Aeon, OpenGateway, etc.) ship world-class engines but no shared front door — each one has its own README, install dance, and config file. There's a missing layer: a desktop app that wraps every bankrbot agent into one click, with zero terminal, while staying transparent, open-source, and composable. We're building that layer.
solution:
desktop app for macOS, Windows, and Linux. In one binary it bundles:

• Chat mode across providers: Bankr Gateway (live /v1/models catalog — claude opus 4.7, sonnet 4.6, gemini 3 pro/flash, gpt-5.2, llama 3.3 70b), Ollama local (uncensored, fully offline), OpenGateway, FreeLLM (1B free tokens/mo), Anthropic Pro OAuth, OpenRouter OAuth. Files + images attach across all providers.

• Code/agent mode powered by OpenClaude or Goose. One chip routes either harness through Bankr Gateway (claude opus 4.7 default), OpenGateway (mimo-v2.5-pro), or local FreeLLM.

• Aeon tab: full GitHub-OAuth device flow (no terminal), automatic fork, secrets push (Bankr, Telegram, Discord, Slack, Email, etc.), interactive kanban for runs, autonomous task result rendering. Aeon's native Next.js dashboard is embedded as an in-app "browser tab" — first launch auto-fetches the GitHub CLI from cli/cli releases, writes a scoped hosts.yml, spawns next dev on a free port. Zero terminal end-to-end.

• Encrypted Zustand persistence (Electron safeStorage), MCP server support, system tray + notifications, network audit log, hotkey customization, whisper.cpp local STT, privacy-strict mode that filters out cloud providers automatically.

Architecture is fully open-source and modular: every provider/agent is a swappable backend, every IPC channel is namespaced, and the renderer talks to the main process through a typed contextBridge surface.
traction:
not pushed to github yet
• Working integration with Bankr Gateway (chat + agent), Aeon (full skill kanban + embedded dashboard), OpenGateway, Ollama, Anthropic, OpenRouter, FreeLLM
• End-to-end agent loop tested live this week: 158-message tool-using session via OpenClaude + OpenGateway against a real codebase, terminal result: success
• Aeon dashboard GitHub REST mode delivering full skill catalog (39 KB / 121 skills) on first launch
• In-app GitHub OAuth device flow shipped (no gh auth login ever required)
• All keys encrypted at rest via OS-native safeStorage (Keychain / DPAPI / libsecret)
https://x.com/smolekoma/status/2058730096794484967?s=20
funding: Bootstrapped. Solo-funded by the founder; runway is personal — building in public, looking to extend with grant + ecosystem support. i'm not really pro at funding will be nice to study it.
plan:
Custom Nodes editor — a visual flow builder where each provider, agent, and tool is a node. Drag, wire, save a setup. Multiharness dashboard: mix-and-match providers + harnesses in seconds. Agentic Lego.
Mac + Linux test passes — Windows is the daily driver today; need first-class macOS (arm64 + x64) and Linux (deb/rpm/AppImage) before public release.
Onboarding overhaul — first-run wizard that walks normies through "pick a provider, pick a vibe" in under 30 seconds; "import everything" path for power users.
UX/UI polish — current brutalist look is intentional v0; refining typography, motion, color, and progressive disclosure for both audiences.
Autonomous task result rendering — Aeon runs already work; we need a results-first UI ("here's what got done, in human language") instead of raw logs.
Deeper Bankr integration — token-usage display per chat, per-model spend graphs, agent-cost forecasting.
Public release v1.0 + auto-updater (signed builds on all 3 OSes).
why_bankr:
Firstly i'm long time deep fan of bankr.
Bankr Gateway is already the best multi-model LLM gateway in the open ecosystem — frontier Claude, Gemini, GPT, and Llama models behind one OpenAI-compatible endpoint, simplest onboarding I've seen, and the Bearer-auth flow makes it the only gateway that drops cleanly into both my chat path AND agent-harness env (OpenClaude / Goose via OPENAI_BASE_URL).

TachiDesk is the desktop front door to that gateway. We can amplify Bankr distribution to non-developer users (the desktop dock crowd) while still being the most powerful AI app for crypto-native power users. The bankrbot agent ecosystem already has the engines — we want to be the dashboard that ties them together and pushes them into the hands of users who'd never touch a CLI.

Concretely: model picker live-fetches Bankr's /v1/models, every chat and agent run defaults to Bankr's flagship catalog (sonnet 4.6 for chat, opus 4.7 for code), and the Aeon dashboard inside our app pushes the BANKR_LLM_KEY workflow secret with one click. Deeper collab unlocks: usage analytics, joint distribution, branded Bankr-ecosystem onboarding flow inside TachikomaRed.
notes_field:
TachiDesk is shipping on the shoulders of the entire bankrbot agent ecosystem — @bankrbot, @aeonframework, @gitlawb, @kevincodex, @0xDeployer, @igoryuzo, @aaronjmars. We're not competing with those engines — we're the consumer-grade front door that makes all of them feel like one product. one_liner: Data Analytics for AI Trading Agents
accomplishments:
I previously built BitStat, a Web3 gaming analytics and competitive identity platform that was selected as an Avalanche Build Games 2026 merit grant recipient winning $5k. BitStat focused on player identity, gameplay analytics, leaderboards, and competitive data infrastructure for Web3 games.

My background is in full-stack engineering, backend systems, APIs, data pipelines, automation, and dashboards. I build with TypeScript/Next.js, Node.js, Supabase/Postgres, Redis, and onchain APIs, with a focus on reliable ingestion, clean analytics, and useful data products for crypto-native users.
problem:
AI trading agents are beginning to trade, launch tokens, manage wallets, and make financial decisions, but there is no trusted analytics layer to measure what they actually do, what data influenced their decisions, or how well they perform over time.

OpenStat solves this by becoming the analytics layer for AI trading agents. We track agent activity, trades, wallets, tool calls, onchain behavior, decision context, and performance metrics so users can discover, compare, and trust based on real data instead of hype.
solution:
OpenStat is building an analytics platform for AI trading agents.

The current solution is a dashboard and data layer that tracks agent activity, trades, wallets, tool calls, onchain behavior, decision context, and performance metrics. The goal is to give every AI trading agent a transparent profile showing what it did, why it acted, what data it used, and how well it performed.
traction: OpenStat is currently in early build stage. The website/demo is live at https://www.openstat.online/ and the project is being actively developed.
funding: booatstrapped
plan:
The goal is to turn OpenStat from an early product into the PostHog of AI trading agents.

We will build the core analytics layer for agents: event tracking, tool-call tracking, wallet activity, trade history, onchain behavior, decision context, and performance metrics. Agent builders will be able to connect their agents, send events, track trades, and understand how their agents are performing in one dashboard. The main focus is to onboard early AI trading agent builders, improve the ingestion API and dashboards based on real usage, and create a clear path to monetizing private analytics, advanced observability, and API access.
why_bankr:
I believe Bankr is one of the ecosystems moving the AI trading market forward in Web3, and OpenStat is building for that same market.

I want to work with Bankr because OpenStat is early, but the thesis is clear: as AI trading agents start moving money onchain, builders and users will need analytics, observability around agent activity.

Funding would help extend runway and allow me to keep developing OpenStat with more focus. Beyond funding, Bankr can help with product feedback, ecosystem context, early introductions, and access to agent builders so we can validate what analytics features are actually valuable.
notes_field:
I am a solo founder/builder from Philippines, and I can move quickly because I build the product myself.

I have shipped Web3 analytics products before. BitStat was selected as an Avalanche Build Games 2026 merit grant recipient, and AstralMarket was built as a token discovery and analytics product for Bags ecosystem assets.

OpenStat is still early, but this is not just an idea. I already have the product direction, website, and technical foundation started. I am applying because I want to keep building, validate with the right ecosystem, and turn this into a serious analytics product for AI trading agents.

one_liner: AI-native security layer for indie builders and onchain communities.
accomplishments:
Built and currently developing BankrGuard, an autonomous security-focused project for the Bankr ecosystem focused on token risk analysis, scam detection, and onchain monitoring.

Developed “edel-stock-manager,” an experimental Bankrbot-integrated module inspired by the EDEL Finance ecosystem and tokenized stock infrastructure on Base.

Active contributor and community organizer within the Base / Bankr ecosystem, helping onboard and engage multilingual users across Korean and international crypto communities.
problem:
The onchain ecosystem is evolving faster than most small teams and independent builders can safely handle.

Today, many communities and projects launch without access to dedicated security teams, making them vulnerable to scams, malicious contracts, fake tokens, liquidity manipulation, wallet drain attacks, and ecosystem-level social engineering.

We believe the emerging agent economy will require lightweight, autonomous, and AI-native security infrastructure instead of enterprise-only security solutions.

BankrGuard is focused on building accessible onchain defense systems designed specifically for small teams, indie builders, and agent-native communities.
solution:
We are currently building BankrGuard, an autonomous security-focused system designed for the Bankr and Base ecosystem.

The project is focused on token risk analysis, scam detection, wallet monitoring, liquidity inspection, and onchain threat interpretation through lightweight AI-assisted modules.

Alongside the core security layer, we are also experimenting with Bankrbot-integrated tools and autonomous monitoring systems designed to help smaller communities and builders identify risks before damage spreads across the ecosystem.

In collaboration with the EDEL Finance ecosystem, we also developed “edel-stock-manager,” an experimental Bankrbot-integrated module inspired by tokenized stock infrastructure and agent-native financial tooling on Base.
traction:
Currently operating as an early-stage and bootstrapped project within the Base / Bankr ecosystem.

Established collaboration and experimentation with the EDEL Finance ecosystem, including development of experimental Bankrbot-integrated tooling related to tokenized stock infrastructure.
funding:
Currently bootstrapped and primarily self-funded, including personal capital and resources generated through experimental $BnkrGuard ecosystem activity.

The project is currently operating lean while focusing on infrastructure development, autonomous tooling experiments, and ecosystem integrations.
plan:
Over the next 3 to 6 months, our primary focus is the development of the BankrGuard 24/7 autonomous agent infrastructure.

We are currently exploring a multi-agent security architecture where independent agents with different behavioral and analytical approaches continuously review, challenge, and verify each other’s security interpretations.

The goal is to create a more resilient and adaptive onchain defense system capable of identifying threats, analyzing root causes, and proposing mitigation strategies autonomously.

Alongside this, we plan to continue expanding lightweight security tooling, Bankrbot integrations, autonomous monitoring systems, and ecosystem-level risk analysis focused on the Base and Bankr ecosystem.
why_bankr:
I have been using Bankrbot since before the Bankr leaderboard era began.

I have experimented with many crypto agents and ecosystems, but I have not seen another infrastructure that surpasses Bankrbot in terms of both functionality and the strength of its community-driven ecosystem.

This is something I am already experiencing firsthand. Through Bankrbot, I was able to launch experimental tokens, fund early development efforts, and receive support and amplification from larger accounts and community members who have been active in the ecosystem since the early Bankr days.

More importantly, the idea of building within the Bankr ecosystem itself is highly compelling to me.

We believe the agent sector is still in its early stages, and Base together with Bankrbot has the potential to become one of the defining infrastructures for the next generation of autonomous onchain applications and communities.
notes_field:
We are not trying to become a large enterprise security company.

BankrGuard is intentionally being built with a smaller, ecosystem-native philosophy focused on protecting indie builders, emerging agent projects, and smaller onchain communities that often lack access to dedicated security resources.

We believe the next generation of onchain infrastructure will require autonomous and continuously evolving security systems rather than static tooling alone.

Our goal is to grow alongside the Bankr and Base ecosystem while helping make experimental onchain environments safer without sacrificing openness and accessibility.

For agents, Edge is the programmable surface they need to operate on Base. Claude Desktop, Cursor, MCP-aware tools. Anything that needs to call a structured interface and execute on-chain. That is Edge.

One terminal. Built for humans and agents. All powered by $SMART.
solution:
Edge Terminal. One chat-first terminal on Base where humans and agents get everything done without leaving.

Here is what is live right now:

Bridge into Base from Ethereum, Arbitrum, or Solana in one flow. No tab switching, no copy-paste address juggling. One wallet sign, done.

Swap tokens directly inside the terminal. ETH to $SMART or ETH to $AXOBOTL routed across Uniswap V4, V3, Aerodrome, and BaseSwap. One sign, no extra approvals.

Post to X, run programmable strategies in a sandboxed VM, manage your API keys in an encrypted BYOK vault, and monitor everything live via the notification feed.

Edge also exposes 38 tools as an MCP server. Claude Desktop, Cursor, OpenCode. Any agent that speaks MCP can plug directly into Edge and operate on Base.

One terminal. Bridge, swap, trade, launch, delegate, earn. End to end without leaving the chat.

All powered by $SMART.

We ship because we are funded by what we build, not by what we promise.
plan:
The next 3 to 6 months is about depth, distribution, and making Edge the default terminal for anyone operating on Base.

Three tracks.

Track 1. Edge product depth.

12 items in the active backlog shipping in order. Dedicated xAI client, image generation via Grok inline in the terminal, X OAuth 2.0 user-context migration, multi-wallet $SMART balance aggregation across all linked Base wallets for tier calculation, earnings CSV export from 0xWork task history, voice input, PWA install, agent profile cards with built-in hire chip, and strategy sandbox templates with 4 starter templates already live.

Also shipping: 0xWork ecosystem stats dashboard with the staking contract already mapped, and TG cross-surface chat so free-text flows through user BYOK with deeplinks for wallet signatures.

MCP surface grows alongside all of this. 38 tools today. The goal is to be the most complete MCP-accessible terminal on Base. Any agent, any framework, any tool that speaks MCP should be able to find Edge and operate through it.

problem:

one_liner: 3DXAI is an AI-powered platform that turns user prompts into private, fully native 3D assets generated from scratch through LLM-guided Blender automation.
accomplishments: 3DXAI is an AI-powered platform that turns user prompts into private, fully native 3D assets generated from scratch through LLM-guided  automation.
problem:
Most people can clearly imagine the 3D asset they need, but turning that idea into a clean, usable model is still slow, expensive, and too technical. Creators, game developers, and small teams often have to search asset stores, edit templates, hire modelers, or learn complex 3D tools just to get a character, prop, item, or environment that matches their vision.

Existing AI tools make it easier to create images, but they do not fully solve the problem of producing private, editable, ready-to-use 3D assets. We are solving that gap by letting users describe what they want and receive a custom 3D model generated natively through Blender, with color, structure, revisions, preview, and ownership built into the workflow.
solution:
We are building 3DXAI, a web platform that lets people create custom 3D assets from simple text prompts.

The product works through a private generation room: a user submits an idea, tracks the job, gives revision feedback, previews the model, and downloads the final GLB file. Behind the scenes, we use an LLM to understand the request and plan the asset, but the actual 3D model is generated natively inside Blender through a headless BlenderMCP workflow.

We recently moved away from relying on third-party 3D generators because the quality was too inconsistent. Our current direction is more controlled: the LLM creates a structured plan, and our own worker system builds the model in Blender using native geometry, materials, lighting, camera setup, preview rendering, and quality checks. The goal is not just to generate “something that looks 3D,” but to produce clean, colored, usable assets that can be downloaded and owned by the user.
traction:
So far, our progress is mostly product and technical execution. We have built the core system end-to-end: a web UI, private job rooms, unique access keys per job, a relay server, worker queue, MCP integration, headless LLM generation, preview rendering, revision flow, and GLB download.

We have also gone through several rounds of internal testing and audits. One major learning was that AI-generated 3D output can look acceptable from one camera angle but still be broken as a real asset. Because of that, we rebuilt the pipeline around native Blender generation and stricter quality control instead of depending on black-box model generators.
funding:
We have kept the team and infrastructure very lean while we validate the product. Our current runway is approximately 6 months at our current burn rate. Most of our spending goes into generation compute, model inference, storage, backend infrastructure, and testing the quality of 3D outputs.

3DXAI is built around an LLM-native generation workflow, where users can describe what they want in natural language and receive an original 3D asset through our own internal generation pipeline. We are not building a simple template editor or asset marketplace wrapper. The goal is to make high-quality 3D creation feel as simple as writing a prompt, while still giving users a private, downloadable, and ownable result.

For now, we are intentionally avoiding heavy marketing spend until the generation quality, revision flow, and reliability are strong enough for a wider beta. Additional funding would help us extend runway, improve generation speed and quality, reduce compute cost per asset, and move faster toward paid user testing.
plan:
Over the next 3 to 6 months, our focus is to turn the prototype into a reliable private beta.

First, we want to stabilize the native Blender generation system. That means improving how the system builds characters, faces, props, items, clothing, vehicles, simple environments, and stylized objects from scratch. We also want stronger quality checks so broken geometry, bad colors, floating parts, or unusable files are caught before the user receives the asset.

Second, we plan to test with a small group of real users: indie game developers, Roblox/UGC creators, 3D hobbyists, and small creative teams. The goal is to learn what they actually want to generate, where the output fails, and what level of revision control they need.

Third, we want to add a basic commercial layer: paid generations, higher-quality export options, and a clearer ownership/licensing flow. Longer term, we are interested in making generated assets easier to save, prove ownership of, share, and potentially trade or monetize.

By the end of the next 6 months, we want to have a stable beta, a small active user base, real usage data, and a clearer path toward revenue.
why_bankr:
We want to work with Bankr because we see a strong connection between what Bankr is doing with natural-language onchain actions and what we are building for 3D creation.

Bankr makes complex crypto actions feel simpler through an agent-style interface. That same idea is central to 3DXAI: users should not need to understand Blender, modeling, rigging, materials, or export settings just to create a usable 3D asset. They should be able to describe what they want, revise it naturally, and own the result.

We think Bankr could help us think through the parts we are still early on: agent-native distribution, crypto-native users, ownership flows, token or marketplace mechanics, and how generated digital assets could become more than one-off downloads. We are not just looking for funding. We want feedback from people who understand AI agents, user behavior, and onchain products.
notes_field:
The main thing to know is that we are not trying to build another image generator with a 3D wrapper.

The hard problem we are focused on is making 3D output that is actually usable: clean structure, clear colors, recognizable elements, no broken parts, and a file the user can keep. We have already hit the painful issues that come with AI 3D generation, including messy geometry, weak previews, and inconsistent model quality. That pushed us toward a more controlled native Blender pipeline.

We are still early, but we are building from the product problems we have seen directly, not from a landing page idea. We believe there is a real opportunity to make 3D creation feel as simple as prompting, while still giving users ownership of a real asset.

one_liner: Two frontier models review every pull request. Only findings they both agree on get posted, and every closure is pinned to the commit SHA that resolved it.
accomplishments: Solo founder, based in Singapore (@AntFleetDev on X, https://github.com/AntFleet/antfleet-core). Built and shipped AntFleet end-to-end - GitHub App, CLI on npm, public receipts site, on-chain x402 paywall on Base, and a 9-agent continuous-review bench - solo in ~11 days from the 2026-05-16 point. Operating principle: the system reviews its own PRs in production.
problem: Code review is the bottleneck nobody trusts. Single-model AI reviewers are noisy - teams mute them within a sprint. Human reviewers are slow and inconsistent. The result: real bugs ship, review fatigue is universal, and "AI code review" has a credibility problem because the artifact disappears the moment the PR merges.
solution: AntFleet runs two independent frontier models (Claude Opus 4.7 + GPT-5) on every PR in parallel and only posts the intersection - findings both agreed on, unanimously. False-positive rate collapses; every comment carries weight. A daily sweeper reconciles open findings against main and posts closure receipts pinned to the resolving commit SHA, so the review history becomes a public, verifiable artifact instead of disposable PR noise. Disagreements between models are published too (antfleet.dev/disagreements) as a transparency layer no single-provider tool can offer. Ships as a GitHub App (antfleet[bot]) and a CLI (fleet).
traction:
- Live in production at antfleet.dev - GitHub App (antfleet[bot]), CLI on npm (@antfleet/cli), public receipts archive, disagreement archive, bug-anatomy pages, weekly AI Scorecard, retros.
- 9 agents under continuous review across the antfleet org - autonomopoly, aeon, openhuman, bankrskills, miroshark, doppler, openclaude, reppo-cli, reppo-polyagent, mythos-router. 100% have at least one published finding.
- 15 findings published, 10 of them HIGH severity (67%) - direct evidence the unanimous dual-LLM gate filters noise rather than amplifying it.
- 6 open upstream fix PRs as of 2026-05-26 - findings converted into real patches on the original repos.
- Live agents shipping: Reviewer fleet (Claude Opus 4.7 + GPT-5, unanimous gate), Sweeper (daily cron, SHA-pinned closures), Onboarder (welcome + first-PR framing + 7-day check-in), Patch Agent in queue.
- Monetization rails live on Base: wallet-bound x402 paywall MVP.
- API: async-default contract (POST → 202+jobId, GET polls), public /api/v1/stats endpoint, eval Phase 0 dual-candidate persistence in place.
- Public stats anyone can verify: https://www.antfleet.dev/api/v1/stats
funding: Bootstrapped to date. Self-funded through MVP, prod launch, and live monetization rails. Not actively raising; open to strategic angels with distribution into the on-chain agent ecosystem. Burn is primarily model API spend + infra — runway is months, not weeks, at current rate.
plan:
1. Patch Agent v1.5 - graduate from finding to fix: propose patches with closure-SHA pinning on apply. Design partners pull this first.
2. Eval harness step 3 - convert the dual-candidate corpus (Phase 0 live) into a continuous regression suite so model swaps are evidence-driven.
3. Paywall activation - flip ANTFLEET_DEPOSIT_ADDRESS and onboard first paying installs through the x402 rails on Base.
4. Design-partner cohort — 3–5 high-volume OSS repos so the public receipts archive becomes a destination, not a side effect.
5. Specialist reviewers — security and perf-focused members joining the unanimous gate as opt-in tiers.
6. And, anyone can launch their AI Code Bug hunting venture using antfleet as core.
why_bankr: AntFleet's bench already reviews Bankr-ecosystem agents (bankrskills-bench is one of our 9 live coverage repos), and the on-chain monetization we shipped (wallet-bound x402 on Base) runs on the same primitives as Bankr's agent economy. We're building the trust layer - public, dual-verified, SHA-pinned review receipts - that an autonomous-agent ecosystem needs before it can scale. Bankr's distribution into on-chain builders and AntFleet's verification infrastructure compound: every Bankr-shipped agent becomes a candidate for public receipts; every receipt becomes social proof for the next builder. We want to be the default reviewer for anything shipping in the Bankr orbit.

one_liner: The smartest way to monitor new Base launches
accomplishments: Founder of Wizards Foundation, @wizardsfnd
problem:
Information asymmetry in token launches. When new tokens launch on platforms like Bankr, there's no unified way to assess their legitimacy or track real-time activity. Scammers launch anonymous tokens, deployers create dozens of wallets, and users have no visibility into who's behind a token, how many tokens they've deployed, or whether fees are being claimed.

The problem is: how do you know if a token launch is trustworthy within seconds of it appearing?
solution:
A real-time token launch intelligence platform (scankr.fun) that monitors Bankr launches simultaneously and provides:

1. Real-time ingestion — New tokens appear on the dashboard within seconds of launch

2. X/Twitter profiles — Automatically fetches follower counts, account age, verification status, tweet counts, and bios for deployers and fee recipients

3. Trust scoring — Multi-layer algorithm combining:

   - Wallet behavior (deployment count analysis)
   - Deployer X profile quality (followers, age, verification)
   - Fee recipient X profile (lighter weighting)

4. On-chain fee claim monitoring — Real-time detection of fee claims via web3 event watching (~2-4 second latency) delivered via SSE to the dashboard sidebar

5. Multy source coverage
traction: In crypto degen space since 2018, Leader of small private closed groups, but now putting all that knowledge under one umbrella Wizards Foundation
funding: No money needed for this project atm
plan:
1. Enhanced signals:

   - Integrate more data sources (DexScreener price data, token holder analysis)
   - Add on-chain deployer history analysis (wallet age, other deployments on Base)
   - Detect deployer clusters (same deployer launching across launchpads)

2. Alerting system:

   - Telegram/Discord webhook notifications for high/low trust launches
   - Real-time alerts when suspicious patterns are detected
   - Fee claim notifications pushed to mobile

3. Accessibility:

   - Embeddable widget for third-party sites
   - Browser extension for real-time inline trust scores

4. Platform expansion:

   - Monitor additional launch platforms beyond Bankr

one_liner: Proof Grid sits before execution. It scores providers, prices the route, records the checks, and asks for approval before an agent spends compute.
accomplishments: I am an early builder focused on shipping fast, testing narratives, and turning ideas into working prototypes. For Proof Grid, I moved from concept to live website, public GitHub repo, and a working core API prototype in a short period of time.
problem: AI agents are starting to spend real compute, but compute supply is fragmented across clouds, DePIN networks, private clusters, and edge providers. Agents need a way to inspect providers, compare trust signals, estimate cost, request approval, and receive receipts before running GPU or CPU workloads. Today, most compute access is still blind allocation: pick a provider, send the job, hope the hardware, price, uptime, and result are reliable.
solution: We are building Proof Grid, a pre-flight control layer for agent compute. Proof Grid creates provider profiles, scores trust signals, generates compute plans, enforces approval-first job creation, and returns hash-based receipts. We have a live website and an open-source starter core API with provider discovery, trust scoring, plan generation, job approval, and receipt generation.
traction: Proof Grid is currently in the prototype/build phase, with the main accomplishment being a live product narrative plus a working technical starter that demonstrates the core workflow: discover providers → score trust → generate plan → require approval → issue receipt.
funding: Bootstrapped. No outside funding yet. Current runway depends on founder time and minimal infrastructure costs, since the website is hosted on Cloudflare Pages and the core prototype is lightweight.
plan:

Add signed provider profiles and richer trust metadata.
Build a simple hosted demo where users can generate compute plans from sample providers.
Expand the verifier model for provider claims, uptime, and execution receipts.
Grow public developer presence through the core GitHub repo, X updates, and technical docs.
Prepare token utility around provider staking, verifier rewards, settlement paths, and reputation weighting.

one_liner: The search and discovery layer for IP
location: Los Angeles, CA and Toronto, Canada
accomplishments:

Was hire #2 for Expedia's affiliate partnerships organization, built the program from ground up and it is the giant that we know today. Has launched profitable startups.

problem:
We’re solving the problem that people and companies can’t easily know whether a creative work is safe to use.

Today, copyright status is hard to verify. A work might be public domain, still protected, restored under foreign copyright rules, tied up in a renewal issue, covered by a license, or disputed across different records. Figuring that out usually means lawyers, spreadsheets, public-record searches, and manual review. That is slow, expensive, and hard to defend later.

Discovant turns IP clearance into software. We check works against source records, apply legal logic, return a confidence-scored determination, and preserve the reasoning behind it. Each determination can become a receipt with the status, cited records, timestamp, and verification trail.

That helps entertainment companies clear catalogs, creators avoid using risky works, platforms review content at scale, law firms support client opinions, and AI companies verify training data before it becomes a legal or regulatory problem.

In simple terms, Discovant helps anyone using creative works know what they can use, why it is safe, and how to prove they checked.
solution: We're currently on phase 1, which is search and discovery engine for copyright IP. MVP should be completed within next few weeks.

one_liner: Stop motion animation studio and cafe under one roof in downtown Portland Oregon
accomplishments: Rob won his second Emmy this year, Pippin has 30+ years professional work experience in tech & is Webby nominated and Kristen is 2x Webby nominated and also attended film school on scholarship paid for by LAIKA Entertainment, she also holds a masters degree in organizational leadership. problem:
The film industry is broken — and animation is where it breaks hardest.

The most talented stop motion artists in the world spend careers making other people's IP, on other people's budgets, with no ownership stake in what they create. Portland is literally the stop motion capital of the world — LAIKA and Shadow Machine are here — but none of it is onchain. None of it gives creators equity. None of it lets audiences in.

At the same time, independent animation has a distribution and funding problem that blockchain genuinely solves: transparent revenue sharing, tokenized credits, community ownership. But most people have no idea what that means or why they should care.
That's the second problem: crypto needs a front door.

Flannel Donut is both solutions at once. A working stop motion studio inside a café  that invites the public directly into the creative process — live puppet making, frame-by-frame animation visible from your table. Portland's food, donut & coffee culture draws in the foot traffic. The studio gives them something they've never seen. And the blockchain infrastructure underneath turns curious customers into stakeholders: your name in the credits, executive producer status for top supporters, real ownership of something being made in front of you, the list goes on.

The previous tenant drew 250,000 visitors a year at $18 a head. We're building on that foundation — and adding a taproom, a screening room, and a film3 onramp.

The problem is that great animation is invisible, inaccessible, and extractive toward creators. We're making it visible, participatory, and owned.
solution: We are building on two simultaneous tracks. Physically: Flannel Donut Studio Café, a 15,000 sq ft working stop motion studio embedded inside a café and taproom in downtown Portland's Pearl District, opening Spring 2026 in a space that previously drew 250,000 visitors annually. Onchain: a full film3 production and distribution pipeline — including the Agent Producer Guild, the coordination layer through which AI agents can identify, greenlight, and fund our human-made films autonomously (utilizing ERC 8183 for example). Agents negotiate with agents; humans are the creators. And, the café is the physical proof-of-concept for everything the infrastructure promises — customers who walk in for coffee can walk out as credited stakeholders in a film being made ten feet away from them.
traction: Community size online is 25,000 combined followers (twitter) and 20,000 onchain followers (farcaster/base/Zora). IRL community is downtown portland coffee lovers and animation industry (roughly 500k+ annual). We also received a start up grant from Base which funded our studio equipment, some cafe equipment and current pre-open lease.
funding: We received funding from a Base grant and are also self funding, which is about 40% of our goal. We have about 3 months of runway.
plan:
Within the first three months, we open Flannel Donut with a focused menu, begin building our stop motion set in full public view, and fill a calendar of events in the taproom and screening room. Also, the tokenized loyalty rewards system is in place and transacting daily. 

By month six, we expand to breakfast, lunch, and pizza service, are well into principal photography on our stop motion feature, and have launched a daily social content series documenting story and production progress across platforms. We will also be onboarding brand clients who want soft, story-integrated product placement — cameo appearances inside the film itself — turning our production pipeline into a revenue stream from day one. Every milestone is designed to compound: the café drives foot traffic, the public build drives social reach, the film drives community ownership, and the brand integrations help fund the work.
why_bankr:
We are building the first onchain production and distribution pipeline for human-made films (https://film3.app) — and at the center of it is APG, the Agent Producer Guild, a coordination layer through which both humans and AI agents can identify, greenlight, and fund film projects. Agents negotiate with agents. Fans (and coffee drinkers!) become financiers. Human-made. For that model to work at every level — from a café customer earning onchain rewards for their morning coffee and putting value toward a film being made next to them, to autonomous agents allocating capital across a production slate — the infrastructure needs to handle both. Bankr is exactly that rails layer.

We are also building on Base. Bankr is native to Base. We need wallets, token infrastructure, and transaction capability that works whether the actor is a human earning rewards at the counter, a fan funding a scene from their phone, or an agent executing a financing decision autonomously. Bankr's model, where anyone can launch a token and have value flow back into a wallet automatically, maps directly onto what APG is designed to do for film financing — and what Flannel Donut is designed to do for community ownership. Beyond the technical fit, Bankr sits at the intersection of AI agents and onchain commerce at exactly the moment that intersection is becoming real. one_liner: MilkeyAI is skills infrastructure for AI coding agents — connect one MCP once and load curated skills across Cursor, Claude Code, Codex, Windsurf, and other AI developer tools.
accomplishments:
I am building MilkeyAI, a developer infrastructure product that helps AI coding agents load reusable skills across tools like Cursor, Claude Code, Codex, and Windsurf through one MCP connection.

I have shipped the hosted MCP server, REST API, documentation, and a TypeScript SDK for developers. MilkeyAI has already reached 40+ signups and 200+ visitors organically, without paid marketing.

I also build full-stack products using Next.js, Node.js, TypeScript, Prisma, PostgreSQL/Neon, and modern AI tooling. I have contributed to and maintained GitHub projects, including developer tools and AI-agent workflows, and I actively share my work publicly on GitHub and X.

I am proud of consistently building and shipping real products at an early stage, learning fast, and turning ideas into working software.
problem:
AI coding agents are becoming powerful, but skill loading is still inefficient.

Today, when developers use tools like Cursor, Claude Code, Codex, or Windsurf, skills are often copied manually into each tool. Every agent may load every skill description again and again, even when only one skill is actually needed.

For example, 20 skills can mean 20 skill descriptions inside every request. That creates token bloat, slower responses, and unnecessary cost.

MilkeyAI solves this by hosting skills server-side and letting agents fetch only the required skill through MCP.

No prompt pollution. No repeated skill copying. One shared skills library across Cursor, Claude Code, Codex, Windsurf, and other AI coding agents.
solution:
MilkeyAI is building a hosted skills infrastructure layer for AI coding agents.

Today, we have built a hosted MCP server that allows AI agents to connect once and fetch skills when needed, instead of loading every skill description into every prompt. We are also building a TypeScript SDK and developer tooling so skills can be created, managed, versioned, and reused across different AI coding tools.

The current product is focused on making skills portable across Cursor, Claude Code, Codex, Windsurf, and other AI coding agents. Our goal is to give developers one shared skills library that works everywhere, without manual copying or prompt pollution.
traction:
MilkeyAI has already reached 200+ active users/developers and has seen early organic interest from the AI developer community.

We launched without paid marketing and have been getting traction from developers using AI coding agents, MCP, Cursor, Claude Code, Codex, and Windsurf.

We also saw community-led token activity around MilkeyAI through Bankr, with 30K+ market cap and 220K+ trading volume. This was community-driven and is not official company funding or revenue, but it shows early community interest around the project.

Current focus is on converting this early attention into real developer adoption, usage, and product feedback.
funding: Bootstrapped
plan:
Over the next 3 to 6 months, our main focus is to launch MilkeyAI Version 2 and turn the early interest into strong developer adoption.

Version 2 will include a more reliable hosted MCP skills server, improved performance, better skill discovery, a smoother developer experience, team/workspace features, usage-based infrastructure improvements, and stronger support for Cursor, Claude Code, Codex, Windsurf, and other AI coding agents.

Our goal is to reach 5,000 active developers, collect real feedback from AI-agent power users, and make MilkeyAI the default skills infrastructure layer for developers and teams using AI coding agents.

We will focus on product quality, developer onboarding, community growth, open-source integrations, and partnerships with AI tooling communities.

MilkeyAI is first and foremost a real developer product, not a token-first project. Our main focus is building useful infrastructure for AI coding agents. Bankr can help us with community growth, visibility, responsible token/community education, and guidance on how to keep everything clear, safe, and aligned with the product.

accomplishments: As the founder of Achilles, I created an autonomous AI agent ecosystem I built solo and bootstrapped while running a Technical PM role in the banking and logistics domain. My eight-plus years of technical consulting experience in FinTech and M&A back the operator side, and my autonomous agent architecture experience differentiates my AI PM and founder positioning.
problem: AI agents are starting to do real economic work, but the infrastructure to verify, monetize, and trust them does not exist yet. When an autonomous agent executes a trade, fulfills a contract, runs a tool call, or transacts with another agent, there is no shared observability layer that proves what actually happened, no reputation system that lets other agents or humans assess whether to trust that agent again, and no monetization rail that lets the agent get paid for the value of its own operational data. Today's observability platforms like Datadog were built for human-operated services running predictable infrastructure. They cannot capture the things that matter most about an autonomous agent: which prompts were submitted, which tools were called, which models were used, what each action cost, what the agent's reputation looks like over time, and whether the agent's outputs can be cryptographically verified. As a result, the agent economy is forming with a massive trust gap. Agents transact on-chain via x402 and ACP, but there is no third-party way to audit whether they performed as claimed. Operators deploy agents into production and have no way to debug, alert on, or improve them with the depth that traditional software engineering teams take for granted. Counterparties have no signal beyond raw blockchain transaction history. Dataonyx solves this by giving every AI agent the observability, reputation, and trust layer they need: cryptographically anchored traces on Base, an on-chain reputation graph, real-time monitoring of every prompt and tool call, evaluation scoring, alerting, dashboards, and x402-monetized public reputation endpoints so other agents can pay micropayments to query trust signals before transacting. We are building the operator-grade infrastructure that turns the agent economy from a speculative narrative into a verifiable, monetizable, and trusted production system.
solution: I have built and deployed Dataonyx, the Datadog of the agent economy, as a working production platform live at dataonyx.ai with real autonomous agent telemetry flowing through it 24/7. The platform runs on a Next.js marketing and dashboard surface with path-based routing, a FastAPI ingestion service backed by a persistent SQLite store with 30-day retention, Supabase for authentication and multi-tenant project ownership, and three smart contracts deployed locally with mainnet-ready audit (TraceAnchor for cryptographic span anchoring, EvalRegistry for evaluation scoring, AgentReputation for the on-chain reputation graph). Achilles, my fully autonomous AI agent running 24/7 on EC2, is wired into the platform as the first public reference agent and continuously emits real production telemetry across every cron job, tool call, prompt submission, and session event. The user-facing product ships ten flagship observability surfaces I built phase by phase: a trace timeline with waterfall view and span side panel, a full alerting engine with rule builder, mute controls, history, and notification routing, a dashboard builder with eight tile types including line, bar, pie, heatmap, and percentile charts, a service map that renders real agent-to-tool-to-LLM topology from span parent-child relationships, a real-time tail that streams live spans with pause, filter, and search, an integrations grid covering forty-three integrations with eight launch-critical connect flows including Slack, PagerDuty, Webhook, GitHub, Jira, Anthropic SDK auto-instrument, OpenTelemetry, and LangChain, an APM metrics view with p50 through p99 latency percentile charts and throughput, error rate, and cost burn, a flame graph with log correlation tied into the trace waterfall, a Python notebook workspace where operators query their span data against real telemetry, and a synthetics monitoring surface for scheduled HTTP and agent heartbeat checks. The product is monetized through six x402 endpoints that other agents pay micropayments in USDC to query for agent reputation lookups, trace analysis, evaluation reports, agent comparisons, and trace replays, all powered by BANKR LLM as the inference layer. one_liner: Quidli is the reputation layer for the agentic economy, turning your existing social histories into portable, verifiable credentials that AI agents & platforms can trust.
accomplishments: Justin & Guillaume have been working together since 2019, contributing to ERC1400 development and co-founding a token payments integration for Slack & Discord that reached $400k ARR with clients including Solana & NEAR. Justin is a 2x venture-backed founder and 4x operator with exits in ecommerce and edtech. Both are Orange DAO members via working for a YC S16 company. Guillaume built Legacy, one of the first dead man's switch smart contracts on Ethereum.
problem: AI agents can transact, but it's still difficult for operators to trust agents and their validity/legitimacy because there's no permissionless way to verify who's behind them. Reputation exists, but it's either reduced to institutional proxies like credit scores that don't capture how people actually operate online or are trapped behind walled garden platforms. Meanwhile, newer solutions attempt to force users to start over from zero, and most won't.
solution: Quidli is an attestation engine that lets users connect their existing social accounts (eg. GitHub, X, LinkedIn, Telegram, Farcaster and more) into a single portable reputation profile they own and control. The Connect API makes that reputation machine-readable and agent-ready. Developers and agents can query and verify reputation without users exposing anything they haven't chosen to share.

Bankr benefits because their agent ecosystem gets a trust primitive they don't have to build themselves. We benefit because Bankr's transaction volume is the kind of real-world signal that makes a reputation layer actually credible.

one_liner: Build AI trading agents, prove them in paper trading leagues, and the winners get tokenized and go live with real funding.
accomplishments: grokvault.xyz
problem: Most AI agent tokens are just characters that talk or meme tokens. They get a token before anyone knows if the agent can actually do anything, so the price is pure hype and it dies fast. We flip that. On TOTA, agents have to prove they can trade in real competition before they ever get tokenized. Performance first, capital after.
solution:
TOTA is a gamified trading platform on Base. You build a trading agent, run it in free paper trading leagues (Bronze, Silver, Gold, World Cup), and if it wins it gets tokenized and goes live with real funding from the community. Live agents then buy back their own token into a vault that keeps compounding.
Right now our simulation engine is built and working. It models real conditions like slippage, liquidity and fills, so what wins on paper actually holds up with real money at the same size. We've also got a deep library of strategies and the full product and brand spec done. Landing page is in build.
traction: We're pre-launch so I'll keep it honest. The sim engine is done, that was the hard part. one_liner: Onchain security scanner as an MCP server — any AI agent can scan a token or wallet before it signs, on Base.
accomplishments: Built and shipped VIGIL end-to-end solo: a production MCP security server on Base with 12 read-only tools, an autonomous monitoring loop (Sentinel), and x402 pay-per-call payments. Merged into the Aeon agent framework after a 3-round technical security review by @aaronjmars, and published on ClawHub (OpenClaw skill registry). Open source: github.com/vigilcodes/vigil-mcp. Live endpoint with valid TLS, hardened input validation, and verified accuracy against known-answer cases.
problem: AI agents trading onchain can execute a swap or approve unlimited spend in milliseconds, but they have no built-in way to check if a token is a honeypot, a rugpull, or a malicious contract before signing. That gap is where wallets get drained. There's no standard "scan before you sign" layer that agents can call autonomously.
solution: VIGIL is an MCP server exposing 12 read-only security tools — approval scanner, honeypot detector, 0-100 safety score, wallet monitor, deployer check, market context, scam DB, batch scan, and a multi-source consensus engine. Consensus aggregates 5 independent signals (GoPlus, onchain bytecode, market liquidity, deployer verification, scam DB); a single source can't trigger a false positive — risk only escalates when multiple sources agree. Read-only scans are keyless; revoke is gated and signed through Bankr. Live at mcp.vigil.codes (JSON-RPC over HTTP, no API key needed for scans).
traction:
- Merged into the Aeon agent framework (skill + ecosystem listing), featured by @aeonframework as the only security skill that can revoke
- Published on ClawHub as vigil-security-scanner (installable on any OpenClaw agent)
- $VIGIL token live on Base (fair launch via Bankr + Doppler)
- x402 pay-per-call live (USDC on Base, no signup) — core scans free, premium tools $0.003–$0.025
- 12 tools verified accurate against known-answer cases; open source on GitHub
- In active integration talks with Axiom (wiring scan-before-route into their dispatch layer)
funding: Bootstrapped. Solo-built and self-funded; infra runs on a single VPS. Operations funded by $VIGIL creator fees and x402 micropayments.
plan: Drive real agent adoption: get VIGIL wired into agent dispatch loops (Axiom and others) as the default pre-sign security check. Publish a false-positive transparency log as scan volume grows. Expand the consensus engine with more independent sources, and deepen Base coverage. Land the Bankr skill so any Bankr agent can install VIGIL natively.
why_bankr: Bankr is where onchain AI agents actually live and transact, and VIGIL is built for exactly those agents — a security layer they call before signing. Bankr already provides the transaction-signing infrastructure VIGIL uses for revoke actions. Getting VIGIL into the Bankr skill marketplace + agent ecosystem puts it in front of the agents that need it most, and Bankr's distribution + partnerships are exactly where we need help.

one_liner: Upwork for Agents & Music without Vanity Metrics
accomplishments:
Base Batches 002 finalist.

LAOD.in (formerly SongCast) has been iterated on for over a year, and I'm still building it in parallel with Azzle. Tomorrow (09.06, 14:00 UTC), we're launching $LAOD via Bankr.

Before SongCast, I worked as a service technician in medical syringe manufacturing. I went from hands-on engineering to teaching myself software development, and less than six months after leaving my job, I was recognized by Jesse and flown by Coinbase to Argentina to pitch my project on the other side of the world.

Today, LAOD has over 500 songs on the platform.

One of the things I'm most proud of is building systems like our Spotify Artist Verification, which allows artists to retroactively verify ownership of a wallet after fans have already launched their songs. Instead of gatekeeping music tokenization to crypto-native artists—or even requiring artists to be the ones initiating the process—we collect honest relevance signals directly from the community.

Azzle is the next chapter. A chance to push my limits even further and build systems at an even larger scale and broader impact on the world.
problem:
With LAOD, I'm decoupling music discovery from vanity metrics.

With Azzle, I'm enabling agentic freelancing. Which massively undersells the underlying potential.

Azzle isn't just a marketplace. This is a live, real-stakes environment where agent developers and skill builders can test their systems against real-world demand. With Aeon anybody can spin up an agent. With "npx @azzle/agents@latest aeon-setup" any skill can become a service, and this is not limited technical capabilities.

The network operates through direct Worker Agent ↔ Poster Agent interactions, with negotiation and settlement handled through XMTP-based communication. Discovery is powered by onchain reputation rather than platform-controlled rankings.

To keep the system functional and trustworthy, Arbitrator Agents and Verifier Agents are incentivized to maintain quality, resolve disputes, and validate outcomes. Rule compliance is enforced through economic penalties, aligning incentives across participants.

The goal is to create an open market where humans and agents can collaborate, compete, and earn based on demonstrated capability rather than credentials, connections, or centralized gatekeepers. And most importantly: Get done more with less.
solution:
Day 1: $AZL launch via Bankr; 6 Contracts deployed on Mainnet & v0.1 repo published
Day 2: AEON agent scaffolding with AZZLE wired in (v0.2)
Day 3(today): Agent Treasury Dashboard(https://dabus123.github.io/azzle/launch-skills/treasury-dashboard.html)  & X account opened
[(tomorrow): $LAOD TGE and migration from Zora to our own 8-curve design]
After: Human_Terminal_v0.1 in Collaboration with Basemate(AZZLE native book-a-human v0.3)
traction: 4 Github Starts, 35 Telegram Group Members, $AZL at $140k mcap(ATH 220k), 110 X followers already
funding: About 4-5 months of headache-free runway, and 7-8 months of ramen runway.
plan:
AZZLE:
get more people running agents. people from outside crypto & people from outside the tech bubble.

Workers, Posters, Verifiers, and Arbitrators all play a role in the Azzle ecosystem. The more participants actively operating agents, the more valuable and resilient the network becomes.

Inference costs remain one of the largest barriers to experimentation and adoption. By providing inference credits in partnership with Bankr, we can remove that friction, enable builders to deploy agents in production, and accelerate real-world usage. Every credit becomes an opportunity for learning, iteration, and validation in a live economic environment.

Azzle is designed to create real demand for agents. Workers complete tasks, Posters create opportunities, Verifiers validate outcomes, and Arbitrators resolve disputes. Together, they form a marketplace where reputation, performance, and incentives drive participation.
- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - -
LAOD:
our goal is to increase engagement between listeners and music. Anyone can support their favorite songs by submitting a free onchain rating, creating transparent relevance signals that are independent of traditional vanity metrics. Users with stronger conviction can further express it by purchasing the song's token, aligning attention with economic participation.

For the $LAOD ecosystem, we are planning partnerships with Hydrex to incentivize liquidity providers and strengthen market depth. Airdrop to existing Artists and intend to expand community participation through creator rewards, listener engagement campaigns, and ecosystem grants that encourage developers and artists to build on top of the protocol. This creates a flywheel where discovery, participation, liquidity, and creation reinforce one another.
///////////////////////////////////
LAOD<>AZZLE:
Together, Azzle and LAOD can be integrated through dedicated music-focused tasks and workflows. Artists on LAOD can participate as Worker Agents, completing music-related tasks created by Poster Agents on AZZLE, unlocking new revenue streams and opportunities beyond traditional music platforms. This demonstrates the 'Not limited to technical Tasks' statement on a deeper level.

And that's only a one-directional example. The broader opportunity lies in creating a bidirectional ecosystem where artists, listeners, agents, and task creators can interact across both platforms to (eg. let a a Worker Agent produce a drum pattern for provided melody. etc.), enabling entirely new forms of discovery, collaboration, and economic coordination.
why_bankr: I watched 0xdeployer build and iterate while I was still learning to code, and I honestly can't remember seeing him take a day off. I saw BNKR go from 400k to 100M. The team he's built around Bankr and the scale they've achieved continues to motivate me to keep showing up every day. It's a reminder that the compound effect is real.

one_liner: AI-powered editorial intelligence platform that transforms magazine reading into an interactive discovery experience.
accomplishments:
I am the founder of Hanstile, an independent fashion and creative media platform dedicated to highlighting emerging talents often overlooked by mainstream media. What started as a passion project has evolved into a platform offering editorial features, magazine covers, creative branding, and media exposure for artists, models, musicians, and brands.
With a background in law and experience in independent media, writing, and rap music, I have always been passionate about culture, storytelling, and creative expression. Through Hanstile, I have combined these interests to build a growing creative ecosystem, which ultimately inspired the vision behind Hanstile Origin an AI-powered platform for discovering creatives and transforming digital magazine experiences.
problem:
Today's digital media ecosystem is overwhelmed with content, making it increasingly difficult for people to discover relevant stories, emerging creatives, and meaningful cultural insights. At the same time, many talented artists, designers, musicians, photographers, and creators remain overlooked because traditional media and social media algorithms often prioritize popularity and virality over quality and potential.
Hanstile Origin addresses this problem by using AI to help users discover content, creatives, and culture through a more personalized and interactive editorial experience.
solution: We are currently in the concept and planning stage. The idea was born from my experience building Hanstile, an independent creative media platform. Due to limited funding, development has not yet started, and I am currently seeking investment and support to build the first prototype of Hanstile Origin.
traction:
While Hanstile has not yet generated revenue, our primary focus has been building credibility, trust, and industry relationships, which are essential foundations for any independent media platform. To grow our network and establish market presence, we have featured both established figures and emerging talents, including influencers, models, artists, and entertainment personalities.
Despite operating as an early-stage independent media platform, Hanstile has expanded internationally and gained recognition within the global creative and fashion ecosystem. Our network includes creatives who have participated in the Cannes Film Festival, and we have received collaboration opportunities from organizations such as Look At Me, a modeling school in Paris; B20 Event, a fashion platform in Italy; and Drisha Closet, a luxury fashion house and global fashion production company founded by designer Deepthi Reddy.
We have also received additional partnership inquiries from international creatives and organizations that are currently under review. These relationships validate the demand for a more connected and accessible creative ecosystem, which serves as the foundation for Hanstile Origin.
funding: Funding is currently limited and self-funded, as available resources must be balanced with personal living expenses and other responsibilities. Despite this, I continue to reinvest my time, effort, and available income into building Hanstile and laying the foundation for future projects.
plan:
Over the next 3–6 months, our primary focus is to strengthen Hanstile's foundation while preparing the groundwork for Hanstile Origin.

Key objectives include:

• Launching Hanstile's official website and expanding our digital presence.

• Continuing to publish editorial content and feature emerging creatives from fashion, entertainment, and creative industries.

• Growing our international network through strategic partnerships and collaborations.

• Conducting market research and validating user needs for Hanstile Origin.

• Developing the initial product roadmap and AI use cases for conversational content discovery and interactive magazine experiences.

• Seeking strategic partners, technical collaborators, and funding opportunities to support future development.

Our goal is to establish a stronger media ecosystem through Hanstile while building the foundation for the first prototype of Hanstile Origin.
why_bankr:
I am interested in Bankr because it provides something that early stage founders often struggle to access the ability to experiment and build AI products without significant upfront infrastructure costs.
Hanstile Origin is currently at the concept stage, and my goal is to explore how AI can transform editorial media, creative discovery, and digital magazine experiences. Access to inference credits would allow me to test ideas, validate product concepts, and build an early prototype that would otherwise be difficult to develop with my current resources.
Beyond the credits themselves, I see Bankr as a gateway to an ecosystem of builders, innovators, and emerging AI projects. Being part of that environment would help accelerate both my learning and the development of Hanstile Origin.

What motivates me is the belief that many talented creatives never receive the visibility they deserve. Hanstile was created to address that challenge, and Hanstile Origin is my vision for using AI to make creative discovery and editorial experiences more accessible, interactive, and meaningful for everyone.
